summary:
True porcelain (developed in China c. 7th–9th centuries AD, with precursors as early as the Han dynasty) was one of China's greatest technological achievements and most globally influential exports — a vitrified ceramic produced by firing kaolin clay at very high temperatures (above 1,200°C), producing translucent white vessels of extraordinary hardness and beauty that were impossible to manufacture anywhere else in the world until European ceramicists cracked the formula in Meissen, Germany in 1708. Chinese porcelain became the most valuable manufactured export in world trade for over a thousand years.

Tang Dynasty porcelain — particularly the white porcelain of the north (Xing ware) and the celadon (green-glazed) wares — established China's global reputation, and 'china' became synonymous with porcelain in English. Song Dynasty porcelain reached even greater refinement: the five great Song kilns (Ru, Guan, Ge, Ding, and Jun) produced pieces prized by emperors and later by collectors worldwide as the apex of ceramic art. Chinese porcelain was traded via the Silk Road and maritime routes to the Islamic world, Africa, and Europe — archaeological sites across East Africa, the Middle East, and Europe yield Chinese ceramic sherds testifying to the extent of this trade. The European obsession with obtaining and then replicating Chinese porcelain drove nearly 200 years of chemical experimentation; the Meissen formula breakthrough (1708 AD) launched the European luxury porcelain industry.
